rulings.Rulings: [Part 5 of 5] The Petitioner shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. Remote appearance is available in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 5 of 5]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MIKE ANGEL LEON
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Disqualify Thomas E. Smurro As Attorney For San Francisco Eagle Bar, Llc And Jesus Alejandro Montiel Urbina
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 5] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Now before the Court is Petitioner and Administrator Evelia Rojas Leon's Motion to Disqualify Thomas E. Smurro as Attorney for San Francisco Eagle Bar, LLC and Jesus Alejandro Montiel Urbina. Petitioner's request, as the Administrator of Mike Angel Leon's estate, that Thomas E. Smurro be disqualified as attorney for the San Francisco Eagle Bar, LLC is GRANTED. Petitioner's request that Thomas E. Smurro be disqualified as attorney for Jesus Alejandro Montiel Urbina in his individual capacity, is DENIED without prejudice. Montiel is currently self-represented in this estate litigation matter. It would be premature to disqualify Smurro as Montiel's counsel on this record. Decedent, Mike Angel Leon, and Jesus Alejandro Montiel Urbana were co-venturers in a limited liability corporation organized on August 15, 2011 as Double Rainbow, LLC, and amended on January 9, 2013 to San Francisco Eagle Bar, LLC (entity number 201122810033). In 2018, Decedent filed a civil action to partition the LLC, and Montiel filed a cross-complaint to enforce the LLC's Operating Agreement's buy-out provision. On May 17, 2019, Mike Leon died before the civil action was concluded. Petitioner was appointed Administrator of Decedent's estate and Letters issued on August 18, 2020. Following Leon's death, Montiel undertook several steps with respect to the venture he co-owned with Leon. On September 18, 2019, Montiel unilaterally filed a Certificate of Cancelation to dissolve San Francisco Eagle Bar LLC (entity number 201122810033). On January 27, 2021, Montiel registered San Francisco Eagle Bar, LLC (entity number 202103210383), listing himself as sole owner and CEO. (Decl. of Kelly, Exhs. B, C). It undisputed that Montiel has continued to operate the underlying business, San Francisco Eagle Bar, located at 398 12th Street, San Francisco, California. A limited-liability company may either be (1) a "member-managed" limited-liability company, or (2) a "manager-managed" limited-liability company. (Corp. Code sections 17701.02(o), 17701.02(q), 17704.07(a).) Per Corporations Code section 17704.07(a), a limited-liability company is a "member-managed" limited-liability company, unless the articles of organization contain the statement required by paragraph (5) of Corp. Code section 17702.01(b): "If the limited liability company is to be manager-managed, the articles of organization shall contain a statement to that effect." A "manager" means a person who, under the operating agreement of a manager-managed limited-liability company, is responsible, alone or in concert with others, for performing the management functions stated in Corporations Code section 17704.07(c). (Corp. Code section 17701.02(n).) The Opposition to the Motion argues that Montiel has retained Smurro to represent the LLC within his legal capacity as the "managing member" of the LLC, supported by the Operating Agreement (attached as exhibit to Declaration of Jesus A. Montiel). The Opposition cites the language at section 1.5 of the Operating Agreement, titled "AUTHORITY OF MEMBERS," to support the assertion that Montiel is the "managing member" of the LLC: "Managing Member acting alone shall have any authority to act for, or to undertake or assume, any obligation, debt, duty or responsibility on behalf of the Company." The Court is unwilling to accept this argument, as framed. [Part 1 of 5]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MIKE ANGEL LEON
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Disqualify Thomas E. Smurro As Attorney For San Francisco Eagle Bar, Llc And Jesus Alejandro Montiel Urbina (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 5] The role of a manager in the Operating Agreement is unclear. On the cover of the agreement, the words "Management By All Members with Officers" appears. In Article III, section 3.1 of the Operating Agreement, titled "MANAGEMENT OF COMPANY," the agreement provides that "all powers of Company shall be exercised by or under the direction of, and the business and affairs of Company shall be managed under the direction of the Members." Later, Section 3.3, "POWERS OF MEMBERS AS MANAGERS," provides that "the Members as Managers shall have all necessary powers to carry out the purposes, business, and objectives of Company, including, but not limited to, the right to enter into and carry out contracts of all kinds; to employ employees, agents, consultants and advisors on behalf of Company; to lend or borrow money and to issue evidence of indebtedness; to bring and defined actions in law or at equity; to buy, own, manage, sell, lease, mortgage, pledge or otherwise acquire or dispose of Company property. [] without limiting the generality of this Subparagraph 3.3A, each Member as Manger shall have power and authority, [sic] to act on behalf of Company" Per the Definition of Terms section 1.12, at page 7 of the Operating Agreement, "Manager" shall mean any Member of Company as authorized by the Articles and the Agreement to manage the affairs of the Company," and "Member" shall mean each person [] who has been admitted to Company as a Member in accordance with the Articles and the Agreement." There is no showing on this record that either Montiel or Leon were "admitted to the Company as members in accordance with the Articles and the Agreement." Per Montiel's declaration at paragraph 2, "this is the Operating Agreement under which the bar has done business ever since [Montiel and Leon acquired the bar] and still does now." The relevance of the Operating Agreement and its application in connection with this motion (and the dispute between the estate and Montiel generally) is far from clear. The Court observes that the only person listed as a "member" and 100% owner of the company governed by this Operating Agreement is John Nikitopoulos. Neither Leon nor Montiel are listed in the Operating Agreement in any capacity. Moreover, although the Operating Agreement refers to the "Articles" throughout - i.e. "the Articles of Organization for the Company filed in the Office of the Secretary of State on August 15, 2011" (Operating Agreement at page 2), no copy of the referenced Articles has been provided to the Court. Construing the Operating Agreement without having the Articles it repeatedly references is challenging. The Opposition argues that Petitioner, as Administrator of the Estate of Mike Angel Leon, holds merely "a financial interest in the company" and that "Neither the Operating Agreement nor the applicable law gives decedent's estate control of or powers in any other part of a Member's interest in an LLC." (Opp. at 2:10, 2:14-15.) This is incorrect. Section 2.11 of the Operating Agreement states, under the title "LEGAL REPRESENTATIVE OR SUCCESSOR OF A MEMBER," "If a Member who is an individual dies [] the executor, administrator, [] or other legal representative may exercise all rights of the Member in the purpose of settling his or her estate or administering his or her property." [Part 2 of 5]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MIKE ANGEL LEON
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Disqualify Thomas E. Smurro As Attorney For San Francisco Eagle Bar, Llc And Jesus Alejandro Montiel Urbina (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 5] Despite the Opposition's argument to the contrary, there is nothing in the record currently before the court designating Montiel as the sole or lone "Managing Member" of the Company, or the required statement in the articles of organization that the limited liability company is to be "manager-managed," taking it out of the statutory "member-managed" default. (Corp. Code section 17702.01(b).) The Operating Agreement does not specifically state that the LLC is to be "manager-managed," meaning "member-managed" remains the default. Further, the Operating Agreement designates all "Members" as "Managers" (see e.g., sections 3.1, and 3.3). This makes the LLC a "member-managed" limited-liability company, as the management and conduct of the limited liability company are vested in the members. (Corp. Code section 17704.07(b)(1).) If there is no voting provision to the contrary in the articles of organization or written operating agreement (Corp. Code section 17704.07(r)(1)) - and there appears to be none here - each member has equal rights in the management and conduct of the limited liability company's activities, including equal voting rights. (Corp. Code section 17704.07(b)(2).) A difference arising among members as to a matter in the ordinary course of the activities of the limited liability company must be decided by a majority of the members. (Corp. Code section 17704.07 (b)(3).) With the exception of mergers, an act outside the ordinary course of the activities of the limited liability company may be undertaken only with the consent of all members. (Corp. Code section 17704.07(b)(4).) For example, the operating agreement of the limited liability company may be amended only with the consent of all members. (Corp. Code section 17704.07 (b)(5).) There is nothing offered in these papers to contradict the Opposition's statement that Montiel hired Smurro "a decade ago [ ] to represent the bar" (Opp. at 1:22-23). There is nothing offered in these papers to show that Leon, as a member of the LLC, disputed Montiel hiring Smurro at that time. However, now, per the Operating Agreement, Petitioner as the Administrator of the Estate of Leon "may exercise all rights of the Member in the purpose of settling his or her estate or administering his or her property," and has exercised the right of a Member to dispute Smurro's representation of the LLC in these estate litigation proceedings. The LLC's adversarial stance toward the Estate in the course of these proceedings hereto is also "outside the ordinary course of the activities of the limited liability company" and so may be undertaken only with the consent of all members. The LLC's "members" are two: Montiel and the Estate of Mike Leon. They disagree on who should be hired to represent the LLC in these proceedings. Jarvis v. Jarvis (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 113 is instructive. As in this matter, the LLC in Jarvis featured a two-person partnership, each member of which owned an equal, 50 percent interest in the partnership. Like the Operating Agreement here, the partnership agreement did not address what to do in case of a decision-making deadlock, and neither partner held the majority required to act unilaterally on behalf of the company against the other member's consent. [Part 3 of 5]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MIKE ANGEL LEON
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Disqualify Thomas E. Smurro As Attorney For San Francisco Eagle Bar, Llc And Jesus Alejandro Montiel Urbina (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 5] One of the members filed an action for partition by sale of land owned by the LLC. The other member hired counsel for himself as an individual defendant, and also unilaterally hired an attorney to represent the LLC. The plaintiff member successfully moved to disqualify the attorney hired to represent the LLC, on the grounds that the attorney was not authorized to act for the partnership, because the defendant member had hired him without the requisite majority consent of the partners. The court of review upheld the trial court. It found that the motion to disqualify was not based on the usual issue of conflicts of interest, which "commonly arise in one of two factual contexts: (1) in cases of successive representation, where an attorney seeks to represent a client with interests that are potentially adverse to a former client of the attorney; and (2) in cases of simultaneous representation, where an attorney seeks to represent in a single action multiple parties with potentially adverse interests." (Id. at 130.) Rather, the issue was the attorney's "lack of authority ab initio to act for the Partnership, and how to resolve a conflict of authority within the Partnership that renders the attorney incapable of acting on the matter in dispute." (Id. at 135). As stated by the California Supreme Court, and relied upon in Jarvis, A trial court's authority to disqualify an attorney derives from the power inherent in every court "[t]o control in furtherance of justice, the conduct of its ministerial officers, and of all other persons in any manner connected with a judicial proceeding before it, in every matter pertaining thereto." (Code Civ. Proc. section 128(a)(5); [case citations].) Ultimately, disqualification motions involve a conflict between the clients' right to counsel of their choice and the need to maintain ethical standards of professional responsibility. [citation.] The paramount concern must be to preserve public trust in the scrupulous administration of justice and the integrity of the bar. People ex rel. Dep't of Corps. v. SpeeDee Oil Change Sys., Inc. (1999) 20 Cal. 4th 1135, 1145-46. An attorney representing a company has a duty to competently represent the entity as a client, acting in the company's best interests. In the face of deadlocked equal members, the attorney cannot abdicate that duty. The deadlock leaves the attorney in an ethical quandary: the attorney cannot act without the client entity's authorization, nor can the attorney take over decision making for the client, absent authority to do so. Petitioner has raised legitimate points regarding Smurro's duty of loyalty with respect to his representation of the LLC. Despite the Opposition's statements that Smurro represents the LLC, and that "there are no issues in this proceeding in which the LLC needs to take a position" (at 5:25-26), Petitioner has demonstrated that Smurro, directed by Montiel in his role as a member manager, may advance Montiel's interests over both the LLC's and the Estate's; that his representation may not be in the best interests of the LLC; that Smurro may be called to testify as a witness in these proceedings; that his representation may unnecessarily deplete the LLC's assets in the course of these proceedings, and may adversely impact the Estate's ownership interest in the LLC. A review of the record of this case will find ample support for these conclusions. [Part 4 of 5]
